candidates.title: Cross-Domain Absorption Characterization with Rate-Distortion Diagnostics and Confound Decomposition
candidates.summary: Three-pronged study: (1) first cross-domain absorption characterization on Gemma 2 2B across 5 hierarchy types with calibrated controls, grounded in pilot evidence showing domain-dependent rates (0-6.6% for knowledge hierarchies vs 13.4% first-letter); (2) confound decomposition separating hierarchy-driven absorption from hedging, reconstruction error, and metric artifacts, with multi-L0 profiling; (3) rate-distortion theory grounded in the successive refinement theorem, where CMI = I(X; f_parent | f_child) provides the first information-theoretic criterion for absorption necessity. Includes threshold sensitivity analysis, lateral inhibition bifurcation check, scaling surface with architecture covariate.
hypotheses: H1: Absorption >= 5% in at least 2 knowledge hierarchy domains on Gemma 2 2B
H2: Hierarchy-driven absorption > 80% of false negatives at optimal L0
H3: CMI negatively correlates with absorption rate (rho < -0.3)
H4 (secondary): Unsupervised pipeline rho > 0.3 on first-letter validation
H5: Width-L0 interaction significant within architecture
H6: At least one hierarchy property predicts absorption (|rho| > 0.3)
H7: JumpReLU shows more binary absorption than L1 (bifurcation prediction)
candidates.pilot_focus: Completed. First-letter: 13.4% absorption. City-country: 0%. City-continent: 6.5%. City-language: 6.6%. Animal-class: 1.4%. ITAC: no clear separation. Confound: 96.9% hierarchy-driven at L0=22.
key_novelty: First cross-domain absorption on Gemma 2 2B (domain-dependent rates as primary finding)
First rate-distortion theory of absorption via successive refinement theorem (novelty 8/10)
First confound decomposition with multi-L0 profiling
First threshold sensitivity analysis of the Chanin absorption metric
First lateral inhibition bifurcation analysis predicting TopK vs L1 absorption dynamics
key_risks: Cross-domain absorption < 3% everywhere after controls (25% probability)
Controls not calibratable -- shuffled baseline remains > 30% (20% probability)
Unsupervised detection fails (50% probability, now secondary)

perspectives_weighted.theoretical: highest -- rate-distortion diagnostic is most novel contribution (8/10)
perspectives_weighted.pragmatist: highest -- engineering feasibility, reusable code, time estimates
perspectives_weighted.empiricist: highest -- statistical rigor, controls, FDR correction, threshold sensitivity
perspectives_weighted.contrarian: high -- confound decomposition, control calibration, honest negatives
perspectives_weighted.innovator: moderate -- conditional cosine retained, SGWA as future direction
perspectives_weighted.interdisciplinary: moderate -- successive refinement theorem, ecological framework, immune escape test

candidates.title: Rethinking Feature Absorption: Decomposing Artifact from Signal with Immune Escape Diagnostics
candidates.summary: Focused metric validation and decomposition study. Threshold sensitivity analysis (5x4 grid), multi-L0 decomposition, immune escape test (zeroing child to see parent recovery), random baseline absorption analysis. Pivot target if cross-domain rates are uniformly near-zero or controls are not calibratable.
hypotheses: H_alt1: Absorption metric CV > 0.3 across threshold grid
H_alt2: Immune escape test shows parent recovery in > 60% of absorption pairs
H_alt3: Hierarchy-driven fraction < 70% at default L0=82
key_novelty: First immune escape test for absorption (zeroing child, measuring parent recovery)
First systematic threshold sensitivity analysis of Chanin metric
key_risks: Immune escape test requires precise activation patching implementation
candidates.pivot_trigger: Cross-domain absorption < 3% everywhere AND/OR shuffled controls > 30% after all refinements

candidates.title: The Rate-Distortion Theory of Feature Absorption: When Is Absorption Information-Theoretically Necessary?
candidates.summary: Theory-first paper applying rate-distortion framework and lateral inhibition dynamics to SAE absorption. Core contribution: Theorem 1 (CMI threshold for absorption optimality) + Proposition 2 (LCA bifurcation analysis). Validated on first-letter. Proposes CMI regularization as design principle.
hypotheses: H_rd1: CMI negatively correlates with absorption (rho < -0.3)
H_rd2: Geometric constant c(w_P, w_C) modulates absorption threshold
H_rd3: Inhibition ratio q > 1 predicts per-token false negatives
H_rd4: JumpReLU shows more binary absorption than L1
H_rd5: CMI threshold predicts L0 ~ 7-14 phase transition
candidates.pilot_focus: CMI estimation on 25 first-letter features (30 min)
key_novelty: First application of successive refinement theorem to SAEs
First quantitative CMI threshold for absorption optimality
First lateral inhibition bifurcation analysis for SAE absorption
CMI regularization as design principle for absorption-resistant SAEs
key_risks: CMI estimation noisy in high dimensions
candidates.pivot_trigger: H3 shows promise (rho < -0.3) but cross-domain empirical results weak (all < 3%)

dropped.title: Fundamental Limits and Identifiability Conditions for Feature Absorption Under Hierarchical Feature Structure
dropped.reason: Novelty score 5/10. Theoretical landscape saturated by Cui et al. (2506.15963), Tang et al. (2512.05534), and arXiv:2506.14002. Proposed bound likely too loose (empirical 15-35% vs predicted >67%). Dropped per novelty report recommendation.

dropped.title: Cross-Domain Characterization and Unsupervised Detection of Feature Absorption (prior front-runner)
dropped.reason: Superseded by cand_cross_domain_ratedistortion. Unsupervised detection de-prioritized after pilot ITAC evidence showed no clear separation. Rate-distortion theory elevated from secondary to co-primary. Cross-domain empirical work retained with adjusted expectations (>= 5% threshold, not >= 10%).

key_revisions: H1 threshold lowered from 10% to 5% based on pilot
Control calibration added as explicit requirement
Domain-dependent absorption rates framed as primary finding
